fingerprint: Globe's axial obliquity (23.44°/23.45°) hardcoded as direct input in dome coordinate system and solar formula. Functional form (sinusoidal declination) is spherical geometry signature incompatible with flat-disc arctangent.
globe_value: ε = 23.4393° (J2000.0 Earth axial obliquity), approximated as 23.44° or 23.45° in standard solar engineering formulas
dome_value: 23.44° in V9 coordinate formula: lat_S = 90 − noon_altitude_june + 23.44 (raw-text/04-coordinates.txt line 25). 23.45° in solar declination formula: φ_sun(t) = 23.45° × sin(2π(t−81)/365)
match_precision: exact (23.44° matches ε to 0.02°, well within historical measurement uncertainty)
why_it_matters: Three interlocking fingerprints: (1) CONSTANT: 23.44°/23.45° is Earth's axial tilt — the angle between the equatorial and ecliptic planes. This concept is meaningless on a flat disc with no rotational axis tilted relative to an orbital plane. The dome imports it as a numerical literal with no derivation from disc_radius, H(r), or sun_altitude. (2) FUNCTIONAL FORM: On a flat disc, solar elevation at observer position is θ = arctan(H_sun/d), where d is horizontal distance to sub-solar point — an arctangent of a distance ratio. The globe's solar declination follows δ(t) = ε × sin(ωt) — a sinusoidal function of time. These are mathematically incompatible functional forms. No parameter tuning converts arctan(H/√(r²+r_sun²−2rr_sun cos Δθ)) into ε sin(ωt). The difference is structural, not parametric. (3) COORDINATE EMBEDDING: The dome's own V9 coordinate system uses 23.44° as an explicit numerical constant to convert solar noon observations into dome latitude. This means the globe's obliquity is baked into the dome's fundamental coordinate transform — every location computed through V9 inherits this globe fingerprint.

code_search.not_found_in: gh CLI unavailable this session — cannot search Python source files in dome repo. However, curmudgeon Cycle 1+2 reviews confirmed: no monitoring domain exists for WIN-056 in monitor.py (39 domains), no solar elevation computation in inject_ai_layer.py or predictions.js.
code_search.notes: 23.44° appears ONLY as a literal constant in the coordinate formula. It is not computed from any dome parameter. The dome's AI context page (07-ai-context.txt rule 11) acknowledges the sun/firmament collision (5,733 km > ~4,300 km firmament) and directs AI to label it 'optical illusion' — but does not address why the elevation follows a globe-derived sinusoidal formula.

deeper_analysis.why_sinusoidal_is_diagnostic: The sinusoidal variation δ(t) = ε sin(ωt) is a direct geometric consequence of a tilted spinning sphere orbiting a distant star. As Earth orbits the Sun, the sub-solar latitude oscillates between ±ε because the rotation axis maintains a fixed direction in space (to first order). This produces a sine function with amplitude ε and period = 1 year. On a flat disc with a local sun circling overhead, the 'declination' (sub-solar latitude) would depend on the sun's radial trajectory r_sun(t). For a circular path at radius r₀, every latitude sees the same solar track — no seasonal variation at all. For an elliptical or spiral path, the variation would be an inverse function of the firmament height H(r_sun), not a sine function. The sinusoidal form is a geometric signature that cannot arise from flat-plane geometry.
deeper_analysis.quantitative_test: At latitude 36°N (Chapel Hill), equinox noon elevation on globe = 90° − 36° = 54°. On dome: observer at r ≈ 5,960 km from pole, sub-solar point at r_equator ≈ 10,008 km, horizontal distance d ≈ 4,048 km, θ = arctan(5733/4048) = 54.8°. Superficially close — but this ONLY works for one date. At summer solstice, globe gives 77.45°; dome gives arctan(5733/(5960−r_sun_summer)) which requires knowing r_sun_summer — a value the dome never specifies because it uses the globe formula instead of computing from its own geometry.

suggested_text: WIN-056's solar formula reveals the dome's most fundamental borrowing. The declination φ_sun(t) = 23.45° × sin(2π(t−81)/365) is Earth's axial obliquity projected through a heliocentric orbit — a concept that requires a tilted sphere circling a distant star. The dome's own V9 coordinate system hard-codes 23.44° as a literal constant (lat_S = 90 − noon_altitude + 23.44), embedding globe obliquity into every dome coordinate computation. On a flat disc, solar elevation follows θ = arctan(H_sun/d), an arctangent function incompatible with sinusoidal seasonal variation for any choice of parameters.
